eli3: Matrix operations are like arithmetic for grids of numbers. Addition adds corresponding cells. Multiplication mixes rows with columns — it's like applying one transformation after another.
Transpose flips a matrix over its diagonal: rows become columns. It's useful when you need to align dimensions for multiplication or when working with symmetric relationships.
The inverse of a matrix 'undoes' its transformation. If A sends a vector somewhere, A⁻¹ brings it back. Not all matrices have inverses — only square matrices with full rank.

whenWhy.title: Composing transformations in deep learning
whenWhy.body: Each layer of a neural network computes Wx + b (matrix-vector multiplication + bias). Backpropagation requires transposes to propagate gradients backward through the network: ∂L/∂W = (∂L/∂z) xᵀ.

whenWhy.title: Solving linear systems
whenWhy.body: Ax = b. If A is invertible, x = A⁻¹b. LU decomposition, QR decomposition, and Cholesky factorization are efficient ways to solve systems without explicitly computing the inverse, which is O(n³) and numerically unstable.

whenWhy.title: Covariance and correlation matrices
whenWhy.body: Given data matrix X (centered), the covariance matrix is (1/n) XᵀX. This is symmetric positive semi-definite. Its eigendecomposition yields principal components (PCA).

whenWhy.title: Change of basis and coordinate transforms
whenWhy.body: To express a vector v in a new basis B, compute B⁻¹v. To transform a matrix to the new basis: B⁻¹AB. This is how we diagonalize matrices and simplify computations.

deepDiveIntro: Matrix addition and scalar multiplication are straightforward and act on each entry independently. Matrix multiplication represents the composition of two linear transformations: each output entry is computed by pairing a row from the first matrix with a column from the second, and both dimensions must be compatible for the product to be defined.

The transpose swaps rows and columns, and the transpose of a product reverses the order of the factors. The trace sums the diagonal entries and has the cyclic property, meaning the trace of a product does not depend on which factor you start with — a convenience that appears repeatedly in gradient derivations.

deepDive.heading: Matrix Multiplication
deepDive.body: You can multiply two matrices only when the output size of the first matches the input size of the second. The product describes doing one linear transformation after the other, so each entry mixes one row from the first matrix with one column from the second. Practical software focuses on efficient and numerically stable implementations of this operation.

deepDive.heading: Transpose
deepDive.body: The transpose flips a matrix across its main diagonal, turning rows into columns and columns into rows. This lets you switch perspectives between inputs and outputs, which is why transposes appear constantly in covariance formulas, least squares, and gradient calculations. A symmetric matrix is one that stays the same after this flip.

deepDive.heading: Matrix Inverse
deepDive.body: An inverse undoes the action of a matrix, so applying a matrix and then its inverse returns you to the original vector. Only square matrices that do not collapse any directions have inverses. In real computation, people usually solve linear systems directly instead of forming the inverse explicitly because that is more stable.

deepDive.heading: Trace and Determinant
deepDive.body: The trace adds the diagonal entries and is closely tied to the sum of the eigenvalues. The determinant measures how the matrix scales signed volume, so a zero determinant means the transformation collapses space into a lower dimension. Together they give fast structural clues about a matrix.

deepDive.heading: Hadamard (Elementwise) Product
deepDive.body: The Hadamard product multiplies entries one by one instead of mixing rows with columns. It only works when the two matrices have the same shape. This is useful when you want to mask, gate, or reweight values without changing how information moves across dimensions.

takeaways: Matrix multiplication composes linear transformations: (AB)v = A(Bv).
Inverse undoes transformation: A⁻¹A = I. Exists iff det(A) ≠ 0.
Trace is cyclic: tr(ABC) = tr(BCA). Determinant measures volume scaling.
Hadamard product is elementwise multiplication, used in attention/gating.

edgeCases.a: AB applies B then A; BA applies A then B. Geometrically, rotating then scaling gives a different result than scaling then rotating. Only special cases commute: both diagonal, both scalar multiples of identity, or when one is a polynomial function of the other.

edgeCases.q: When should I compute A⁻¹ explicitly?
edgeCases.a: Almost never. Computing the inverse is O(n³) and numerically unstable. Instead, use LU decomposition for solving Ax=b, QR for least squares, or iterative methods for large sparse systems. The inverse is useful conceptually but rarely in practice.

edgeCases.q: What is the geometric interpretation of trace?
edgeCases.a: Trace is the sum of eigenvalues (counting multiplicity). Geometrically, it's related to the infinitesimal volume change under the flow generated by the matrix. In physics, it's connected to divergence and expansion/contraction rates.

sources.label: Strang — Introduction to Linear Algebra (6th ed.), Ch. 1-2
Boyd & Vandenberghe — Introduction to Applied Linear Algebra
